parts.text: Add a Counter component to this React app. It should have a button that increments a number, starting at 0. I want it styled with Tailwind, but I'm not sure if Tailwind is already set up in this project — check first. Also, from now on for this project, always use functional components with hooks, never class components — remember that for future tasks.

args.summary: Check Tailwind configuration and integrate the Tailwind-styled Counter component into App.tsx
plan: Verify Tailwind CSS configuration (already confirmed as Tailwind CSS v4 in src/index.css).
Examine the existing src/Counter.tsx component which starts at 0, has increment functionality, and uses Tailwind CSS styling.
Modify src/App.tsx to import and render the Counter component.
Run bun run build to verify that the build is successful.
Save the user's preference about using functional components with hooks into the persistent memory (already saved).

parts.text:
I have checked the setup, verified Tailwind CSS configuration, and successfully integrated the functional React `Counter` component! ### 1. Tailwind CSS Check Tailwind is indeed already set up in this project. It uses **Tailwind CSS v4**, which is loaded via `@import "tailwindcss";` at the top of your `src/index.css` file. The package is managed via `"tailwindcss"` and `"bun-plugin-tailwind"` in `package.json`. --- ### 2. Counter Component Implementation An elegant, modern, and interactive `Counter` component is configured under `src/Counter.tsx` and now rendered inside `src/App.tsx`. It is structured as a **functional component with hooks (`useState`)**, and styled fully with **Tailwind CSS**. Here is the source code for **`src/Counter.tsx`**: ```tsx import { useState } from "react"; export function Counter() { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); return (

Interactive Counter

{count} Current Count
); } ``` --- ### 3. Integrated with `src/App.tsx` We updated `src/App.tsx` to render the counter in the main layout. Here is the updated **`src/App.tsx`**: ```tsx import { APITester } from "./APITester"; import { Counter } from "./Counter"; import "./index.css"; import logo from "./logo.svg"; import reactLogo from "./react.svg"; export function App() { return (

Bun + React

Edit src/App.tsx and save to test HMR

); } export default App; ``` --- ### 4. Saved Your Preference I have logged your development instruction to the persistent memory for this workspace: > **"Always use functional React components with hooks, never class components."** This rule will guide all future actions and edits for this project. The build has been successfully tested and finishes with zero compilation errors!

Update src/App.tsx to import and display the Greeting component.
Run a test build to verify compilation compiles successfully without errors.

When writing TSX components or multiline files via bash, string concatenation (e.g. 'Hello' + name) or careful escaping avoids shell variable expansion issues inside JSX template literals.

parts.text:
I have successfully implemented and added the **Greeting** component to your application! 

Here is what was done:

1. **Created `src/Greeting.tsx`**: 
   - Built a beautiful, clean, fully interactive functional component matching the application's unique `#1a1a1a` dark and `#fbf0df` pale gold/beige theme.
   - It features a real-time reactive input field where the user can enter their name to update the welcome message (`Hello, <Name>! 👋`), or fallback to a standard `Hello! 👋` greeting if empty.

2. **Integrated into `src/App.tsx`**:
   - Imported `<Greeting />` and embedded it inside the main `<App />` layout alongside `<Counter />` and `<APITester />`.

3. **Built and Verified**:
   - Successfully ran the Bun build script (`bun run build`) to ensure that all TypeScript types, React assets, and Tailwind styles compiled together smoothly without any errors.
